title: Catechist Guide — Jesus Christ, God and Man
html:
Catechist Guide

For preparing and leading the class. For catechist use only.

Class Summary

Estimated Duration: 75–90 minutes

Central Theme: Jesus of Nazareth is true God and true man; in the union of His two natures (hypostatic union) lies the mystery of our salvation.

Objectives:

  • Recognize Jesus as the eternal Son of God, "begotten, not made, consubstantial with the Father" (Nicaea, CCC 464–465).
  • Understand the full humanity of Christ: "like us in all things but sin" (CCC 470–471).
  • Learn the definition of Chalcedon on the hypostatic union and its importance for the faith (CCC 467).
  • Discover the meaning of kenosis as key to the Incarnation and a model for Christian life (CCC 471–474).
  • Learn to live ordinary life (work, family, routine) as a place of encounter with Christ, in light of the thirty years in Nazareth.
  • Respond to Jesus' question: "But you, who do you say that I am?" with a personal, committed confession.

Key Verse: "And the Word became flesh and dwelt among us" (Jn 1:14)

Catechism References: CCC 422–483

Suggested Class Structure

Section Time What the Catechist Does
1. The Great Question: "Who do you say I am?" 10 min Read Mk 8:27–30. Ask: "Who do people today say Jesus is?" (typical answers: great teacher, prophet, revolutionary, myth...). Then ask the personal question: "And you, who do you say He is?" Listen without judging. If someone says "I don't know," validate it: "That's an excellent starting point."
2. Jesus: The Name That Says It All 10 min Explain the meaning of "Jesus" (God saves), "Christ" (Anointed One), and other titles: Lord, Son of God, Word, Emmanuel. Activity: have each participant research the meaning of their own name and share.
3. True God: Nicaea and the Battle for Divinity 15 min Read Jn 1:1, 14. Tell the story of Arius and Nicaea: why does it matter that Jesus is God, not just the most perfect creature? Present C.S. Lewis's trilemma (madman, liar, or Lord). Emphasize: if Jesus is not God, He cannot save us.
4. True Man: Chalcedon and the Hypostatic Union 12 min Read Heb 4:15. Explain Jesus' real humanity: why do we need Him to be man? The formula of Chalcedon (without confusion, without change, without division, without separation). Use the coin analogy: two sides, one coin.
5. Kenosis: God Emptied Himself for Love 12 min Read Phil 2:6–11 slowly. Explain kenosis as the key to divine love and a model for Christian life. Ask: what does kenosis mean for daily life? Connect with the Annunciation and Mary's fiat.
6. Nazareth: Thirty Years of Ordinary Life 10 min Read Lk 2:51–52. Reflect: God spent most of His earthly life in total ordinariness. What does this tell us about the value of our ordinary lives? Brief activity: "One thing from your daily routine you would do differently."
7. Final Reflection and Prayer 6 min Review the Key Idea. Invite each person to write or silently speak their response to Jesus' question. Close with the prayer from the workbook.

Notes for the Catechist

C.S. Lewis's Trilemma as a Pastoral Tool

Lewis's trilemma (madman, liar, or Lord) is one of the most effective apologetic tools for young people and adults who reduce Jesus to a "great moral teacher." The force of the argument is simple: someone who claims to be God cannot be merely a good teacher. He is either lying, insane, or who He says He is. The catechist's question should be: "Which option seems most reasonable, given the Gospels?" This isn't about "winning" an argument but about leading the person to the threshold of faith. Faith is a gift, but reason prepares the way.

Nicaea and Chalcedon: Why Conciliar Christology Matters

The first four Ecumenical Councils (Nicaea 325, Constantinople 381, Ephesus 431, Chalcedon 451) are the Church's response to Christological errors that, if accepted, would destroy the faith. Each council answers a specific question: (1) Is Jesus true God? Yes, consubstantial with the Father (Nicaea). (2) Is the Holy Spirit true God? Yes (Constantinople). (3) Is Mary Mother of God? Yes, because the One born of her is God (Ephesus). (4) How are divinity and humanity united in Christ? In one Person, two natures, without confusion, without change, without division, without separation (Chalcedon).

A useful teaching aid: draw the heresies as deviations from orthodoxy. Docetism says Jesus only appeared human (too divine). Arianism says Jesus is not true God (too human). Nestorianism separates the divine and human so much that there are practically two persons. Monophysitism mixes the natures into one. Orthodoxy is the balance defined by Chalcedon: two natures, one Person.

Kenosis as the Key to Everyday Holiness

Kenosis is not just a theological concept: it is the heart of Christian spirituality. St. Paul says: "Have this mind among yourselves, which is yours in Christ Jesus" (Phil 2:5), and then develops the kenosis hymn. The imitation of Christ begins here: emptying oneself of ego, pride, need for control, and the pretense of always being right. Emptying so that God may fill. Dying to oneself so that Christ may live in us. This is the dynamic of holiness: not a conquest, but a stripping away.

The catechist can ask: "Is there something in your life you need to let go of so that God has space?" The question must be concrete, not generic.

The Hidden Life of Nazareth: Recovering the Holiness of the Ordinary

One of the most striking aspects of this class is the fact of the thirty years in Nazareth. Many people live with the feeling that their "normal" life is of no interest to God, that what is truly spiritual happens somewhere else (a retreat, a convent, a mission). Jesus refutes this with thirty years of workshop, saw, sawdust, difficult customers, and family meals. God chose to live like this. Therefore holiness is not elsewhere: it hides in the workday, in the family meal, in daily duties.

St. Josemaría Escrivá preached this forcefully: "God waits for you in the ordinary." The class can close with a very concrete invitation: this week, live your routine as if God were at your side — because He is.

Connecting with Incarnational Spirituality

The Incarnation is not only a historical event two thousand years ago. It is the permanent shape of God's relationship with the world. God continues to come to us through material things: the sacraments (water, bread, wine, oil), the Church as visible community, the poor in whom Christ is present. An incarnational spirituality does not flee the world but transforms it from within. The catechist can connect this with the theology of St. John Paul II (Theology of the Body) and with the social doctrine of the Church: if God became flesh, the material world matters. The body matters. Social justice matters. The Incarnation is the foundation of a Christian humanism that takes the world seriously.

Adaptation by Age Group

🌱 Seed (Children 7–12)

Use the video game analogy: "If the game's creator appears as a character inside the game, what would happen?" Explain that Jesus is God who entered His own creation. The coin: two sides, one coin = two natures, one Person. Activity: draw the manger scene with a golden circle of light (divinity) surrounding the child (humanity). Memorize Jn 1:14 with gestures. Teach the Sign of the Cross and explain that Jesus' name is so holy we bow our heads when saying it.

🌿 Shoots (Teens 13–17)

Connect with the search for identity characteristic of adolescence. "Who are you?" is the question teens ask daily. "Who is Jesus?" is the question God asks back. Lewis's trilemma is very effective with teens; it forces them to take a position. Use kenosis as counter-culture: in a world that says "be yourself, never lower yourself, never back down," Jesus says "whoever wants to be first must be servant of all." The life of Bl. Carlo Acutis, a millennial teen who used his tech skills to spread Eucharistic devotion, resonates powerfully here.

🌾 Root (Adults New to the Faith)

Many adults approach faith with a "low Christology": Jesus as ethical model, friend, inspiration. The challenge is helping them make the step to full Christological faith: Jesus is not just a model to follow, but God who comes to save us. Moving from "admiring Jesus" to "entrusting life to Jesus" is the step of the catechumenate. The testimony of famous converts (Lewis, Chesterton, St. Augustine) can be a great help.

🌲 Tree (Formed Adults)

Deepen with patristic theology. St. Athanasius: "God became man so that man might become God" (De Incarnatione, 54). St. Irenaeus: "The Word of God became what we are to make us what He is" (Adversus Haereses, V). St. Thomas Aquinas: STh III, qq.1–15 on the fittingness of the Incarnation. Connect with 20th-century theology: Karl Rahner and "anonymous Christianity"; Hans Urs von Balthasar and Trinitarian kenosis. John Paul II's encyclical Redemptor Hominis is a fundamental text.

Supplementary Resources

  • YouCat: Questions 47–55 ("Is Jesus True God?" "Is Jesus True Man?" "What does it mean that Jesus is the Son of God?").
  • Opus Dei: Topic 4, "Jesus, True God and True Man" at opusdei.org, within the "Topics of Christian Faith" series.
  • Video: The Chosen (Dallas Jenkins series, free on its app), especially the Christmas special episode on the infancy and childhood.
  • Art: Byzantine Pantocrator icons. Mosaics of Ravenna (Sant'Apollinare Nuovo, 6th century). El Greco's The Disrobing of Christ (1577–1579).
  • Book: Frank Sheed, Theology for Beginners, chapter on the Incarnation. St. Athanasius, On the Incarnation of the Word (St. Vladimir's Seminary Press, accessible edition).
  • Visual Catechism: Real + True (osvnews.com/real-true), episode "God Became Man."
  • Music: Handel's Messiah; Hillsong's "What a Beautiful Name"; Matt Maher's "Lord, I Need You."

Tough Questions

"Why did God become man? Couldn't He have saved us some other, easier way?"

St. Thomas Aquinas (STh III, q.1, a.2) and St. Anselm (Cur Deus Homo) respond: God, being omnipotent, could have saved us in many ways. He chose this one because it was the most fitting for manifesting His love and His justice. The Incarnation is not the only possible form of salvation, but it is the most beautiful and the most worthy of God: it saves us from the inside, sharing our condition, not from outside as an impersonal decree. Moreover, only a human being could represent humanity and offer a proportionate reparation; only God could offer an infinite reparation. Jesus is the only one who fulfills both conditions (CCC 459–460).

"How can Jesus be 100% God and 100% man? That adds up to 200%."

The coin analogy (two sides, one coin) is visually helpful but limited. Better is the analogy of the Person: divinity and humanity in Christ are not added like quantities; they are united in a single Person. It's like the relationship between soul and body in us: we are not "50% soul and 50% body"; we are one person with two dimensions. In Christ, the Person is divine (the Word), and this divine Person assumes a complete human nature. There is no mathematical addition: there is an ontological union in one hypostasis (CCC 467–468).

"If Jesus was God, why did He pray to the Father? Why didn't He know the day of judgment?"

Jesus possessed two kinds of knowledge: divine knowledge (proper to His divine nature, omniscient) and human knowledge (limited, progressive). In His humanity, He experienced real growth: "Jesus increased in wisdom" (Lk 2:52). Catholic theology speaks of three types of human knowledge in Christ: the beatific vision (immediate knowledge of God), infused knowledge (supernatural knowledge given by God, like the gift of prophecy), and acquired knowledge (normal human knowledge through experience and learning). When Jesus says He doesn't know the day of judgment (Mk 13:32), He is speaking from His human knowledge, which was limited as part of His kenosis. This doesn't contradict His divinity: God freely chooses to submit Himself to human conditions in order to save us from within (CCC 472–474).

"Why does the virginal birth matter? Isn't it just a legend?"

The virginal conception of Jesus is affirmed in both infancy Gospels (Mt 1:18–25; Lk 1:26–38) and in the Creed. Its theological meaning is profound: (1) It manifests that the initiative of the Incarnation is entirely divine, not the fruit of human history. (2) It shows that Jesus is Son of God in the strict sense, not of Joseph. (3) It fulfills the prophecy of Isaiah 7:14: "The virgin shall conceive and bear a son, and shall call his name Immanuel." Denying the virginal birth in practice usually leads to a lowered Christology where Jesus is only an exceptional man. The Church believes Jesus was conceived by the power of the Holy Spirit in the womb of the Virgin Mary, "without the agency of a man" (CCC 496–498, 502–507).

"Is Lewis's trilemma too simplistic? What if the Gospels exaggerated?"

It's true that the trilemma presupposes the historical reliability of the Gospels. If someone objects, "Jesus never claimed to be God; the Church invented that later," the response has two levels: (1) Historical: the Gospels are reliable sources, and Paul's letters (50s–60s AD) already confess Christ's divinity. The gap between Jesus' death (c. 30 AD) and the earliest writings is only twenty years, insufficient for a myth to develop. (2) Existential: Jesus, as all available sources present Him, behaved as someone with divine authority: He forgave sins (something only God can do), claimed to be greater than the Temple and the Sabbath, and accepted being called "My Lord and my God" (Thomas, Jn 20:28). Either all of that is false, or it is true. There is no middle ground, and the available evidence points strongly toward its truth.

"Did Jesus know He was God from childhood?"

Catholic theology responds that yes, from the first instant of His conception, Jesus had full consciousness of His divine identity (through the beatific vision). But this does not eliminate human development: Luke says He "increased in wisdom" (Lk 2:52). Jesus' human consciousness was real and gradual in its expression, although He possessed His divine identity from all eternity. It's somewhat analogous to how a child prodigy "knows" things intrinsically but articulates them progressively (with all the cautions of analogy). The ultimate mystery remains, and it is proper to the kenosis: God chooses to live the human condition in all its dimensions, including psychological growth (CCC 470–474).

Catechist's Prayer

Lord Jesus,
Eternal Word of the Father, true God and true man,
You who emptied Your glory to find me
in my flesh, in my time, in my small story,
teach me to speak of You with the truth of the Creed
but also with the fire of love.
May those who listen not remain in ideas about You
but encounter You, the living Lord.
May my catechesis not be mere human word
but an echo of the Word who became flesh.
And at the end of the class,
may each person be able to answer, like Peter,
not with memorized words
but with a heart on fire:
"You are the Christ, the Son of the living God."
Amen.

Print Catechist Guide
